ctxNote: roof uses shared city-context.json — uniquely rich (climateZone/hailRisk/hurricaneZone/snowLoad/avgHomeAge/permitNote/weatherNote/materialTip/hoaPrevalence). No vertical-specific costDriverNote/redFlagNote here.

currentFAQs.q: How much does a new roof cost in {{CITY}}?
currentFAQs.a: Most homeowners in {{CITY}} pay around {{MOST_COMMON_PRICE}} for a new roof. The full range is ${{AVG_LOW}} to ${{AVG_HIGH}} depending on roof size and materials.

currentFAQs.q: Is roofing more expensive in {{CITY}} than average?
currentFAQs.a: Roofing in {{CITY}} is {{CITY_VS_NATIONAL}} the national average. Local labor rates, material availability, and building code requirements all influence pricing.

currentFAQs.q: Why do roofing quotes vary so much?
currentFAQs.a: Roofing estimates vary depending on labor assumptions, material brands, flashing work, ventilation upgrades, and warranty coverage included in the proposal.

currentFAQs.q: Should I always choose the lowest roofing quote?
currentFAQs.a: Not always. Lower bids sometimes exclude items like flashing, ventilation upgrades, or disposal costs that appear later as change orders.

currentFAQs.q: What should a roofing quote include?
currentFAQs.a: A complete quote should include tear-off, underlayment, flashing, ice and water shield, drip edge, ventilation, ridge vent, starter strip, ridge cap, decking inspection, disposal/cleanup, and the permit. If any of these are missing, ask your contractor why.
